observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to:
 start the reconciliation of those property items included in the inventory
report with the records maintained by the Accounting and the General
Services Offices;
 exert best efforts to complete the required inventory count and the
reconciliation of records for those property items not covered in the
current year’s physical inventory taking;
b. City Accountant to investigate the noted discrepancy/ies between the book balance
and the RPCPPE to fully identify the errors/deficiencies that contributed to the noted
variance, and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted; and
c. Inventory Committee and City Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of
Section 124, Paragraph 2 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, Section 53, Chapter III of
GAAM, Volume III and Paragraph C.3, Chapter 5 of the Manual on Property
Custodianship.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Engineer to fast track the process of identifying the projects recorded in the
Registry of Public Infrastructure that are existing, and reassess the value based on
the guidelines mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008;
b. City Accountant to record in the books of accounts the value of the road network
projects to be identified by the City Engineering Office; and
c. concerned city officials, to henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular No.
2015-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. effect the derecognition of the cost of P1,448,138.25 representing the
demolished structures; and
b. coordinate with the City Engineer in order to identify the costs and to reconcile
the PPE records as regards PPEs that have been replaced due to repair,
renovation, and rehabilitation, then derecognized the amounts of asset
equivalent to the removed parts of any other necessary adjustments, to
comply with Paragraphs 85 of IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a. prepare an adjusting entry to record the corresponding valuation of the year-
end balance of the accountable forms costing at least an amount of
P2,416,205.83; and henceforth,
b. strictly comply with Section 120, Chapter 7 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the coordinating/focal point officer to:

a. ensure the immediate and proper implementation of projects funded by the fund
transfers from SAs;
b. initiate the return of any unused balance of the fund transfers to the SAs, upon
verification of the status of the funds and in case of the completion of the project,
pursuant to the aforecited regulations; and henceforth,
c. strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 in implementing projects
funded by SAs so as to ensure that funds are utilized and liquidated in a timely
manner, and that the services and benefits due to the public are not hampered.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a. coordinate with the CCLO, and issue demand letters to those existing
organizations, and demand immediate settlement of their payables to the City;
b. consider and/or initiate a request for write-off for those balances which
remained dormant for 10 years or more; and henceforth,
c. conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts, to ensure
collection when these become due and demandable, in conformity with COA
Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. enforce the liquidation of the cash advances in accordance with the prescribed
period, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002;
b. require the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances, otherwise,
initiate the filing of the request for authority to write-off the outstanding cash
advances of accountable officers who are no longer in service, to the COA Audit
Team Leader and/or Supervising Auditor, in accordance with COA Circular No.
2016-005; and henceforth,
c. strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and
liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. coordinate with the City Focal Person - DICT Project as regards the intended
recipients of the remaining unreleased computer devices for the proper and
immediate release of the units;
b. provide proper documentation on the previously issued computer laptops and
tablets;
c. review the terms of agreement as regards the release and distribution of the
computer devices, and ensure corrective actions on the issuance made to
recipients not included in the DICT approved list; and henceforth,
d. strictly comply with the provisions of the Terms of Engagement on the roles of
the City as regards the release of the DICT donated computer devices.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. OIC-City Accountant to: a.1 coordinate with the City Engineer to identify and reconcile the improved, rehabilitated, and repaired, then derecognized the amount of the replaced parts. Likewise, continue to reconcile, analyze and investigate, together with General Services Office, the other cause/s of discrepancies between the book balance and the RPCPPE and make necessary adjustments, if warranted; a.2 seek the assistance of the City Engineering Office to identify the improved/replaced portion of the City Hall Building and derecognize the cost of the replaced parts. a.3 review the Schedule of PPE and draw a JEV to record the adjustments in the Depreciation expense and Accumulated Depreciation accounts in the amount of P919,360.78 for the PPE accounts affected as presented in Annex I, to fairly present the financial statements in accordance with PAG 3 of IPSAS 17; a.4 provide residual value estimates to at least five per cent (5%) on the identified PPEs with no residual value provision in accordance with PAG 6 of IPSAS 17; and a.5 exert efforts to locate documents and if turned futile, request for authority to write-off the unreconciled/undocumented accounts without details by following COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the “Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies” to remove negative balances that understates the gross amount of PPE.
observations.managementAction:
Management, thru the City Accountant, the City General Services Officer and the City Procurement Officer reasoned out that through the years, they are implementing various solutions to resolve this perennial issue on the Inventory and Reconciliation of Property, Plant and Equipment. The latest of which is the formulation of an Asset Management Team (AMT) that will cater to all the acquired properties, including but not limited to non-derecognition of the cost of materials replaced on several properties, and reconciliation of entries between the Schedule of PPE and RPCPPE.
The AMT has convened on December 3, 2021, and among the topic discussed was the preparation for the implementation of the one-time cleansing per Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006.
Management also commented that due to the voluminous assets of the City, the inventory count was made in parts, thus only a portion of the report was submitted to the Audit Team and reconciled with the books. Management, however, assured that extra efforts will be exerted to attain the completion of the aforesaid reports.
observations.reason: An Asset Management Team has been created to cater on City’s PPE. Management has yet to identify the amount of unreconciled/undocumented accounts or update for one-time cleansing of PPE account is considered.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. OIC-City Accountant to: base the computation of interest on the maturity dates indicated in the Certificate of Time Deposits and record the earned interest only, instead of the interest for the whole duration of the time deposit to ensure correct computation; b. OIC-City Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher/s (JEVs) to adjust the recorded unearned interest in the amount of P791,401.45 and the unrecorded credit memo amounting P3,976,366.00 for the collection from PhilHealth to fairly present the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office drew JEV No. RCD-2101-005 RCD-2101-009 RCD-300-21-02-025 recognized collection of PhilHealth. While, JEV No.100-2012-4601F and JEV No.100-2102-016 recognized interest income for December 2020.
observations.reason: Interest income for CY 2021 were properly recognized. JEV No.100-2101-579e and JEV No.100-110-56100o. RCD-2101-009 RCD-300-21-02-025 recognized collection of PhilHealth.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. OIC-City Accountant to draw a JEV to record the unrecorded CIP with a debit to CIP-Infrastructure Assets with a debit to CIP-Buildings and Other Structures and credit Accounts Payable by the same amount of P69,795,464.47. Likewise, adjust the erroneous entry in the CIP by debiting Repairs and Maintenance - Infrastructure Assets and crediting CIP by the same amount of P10,738,612.76; b. Office of the City Accountant and Office of the City Engineer to coordinate with each other to compile the records/documents of all ongoing projects to facilitate recording of additions and adjustments, under the appropriate asset accounts for fair presentation of financial statement; and c. General Services Office to henceforth, include Construction in Progress account in the report for RPCPPE on the conduct of physical count of PPE and preparation of RPCPPE.
observations.managementAction: The necessary adjustment has been effected in the books thru JEV No.300-2102-089k and JEV No.014-03-21-200. Likewise, adjust the erroneous entry in the CIP by debiting Repairs and Maintenance - Infrastructure Assets by JEV No.100-2102-089k and JEV No.014-03-21-200. CIP is now monitored and completion of CIP is being monitored and updated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City General Services Officer to: a.1 transfer the amount of P2,189,269.46 from the land account to Road Network account; a.2 include the amount of P7,849,471.50 representing 15% expropriation deposit to the cost of the land; a.3 conduct reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the Accounting records to ensure completion of the said records; a.4 exert effort to locate the missing TCTs for verification/validation with the Registry of Deeds to ensure completion of the said records; a.5 henceforth, ensure that the RPCPPE contains all the details of the property specifically TCT Numbers for easy reference. b. OIC-City Accountant to: b.1 assign personnel to gather documents and records relative to the P15,806,356.62 difference between the Schedule and GL balances. If exerting best efforts to locate the documents and records prove futile, consider the write-off of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the “Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies”.
observations.managementAction: The necessary adjustment has been effected in the books thru JEV No. 100-1101-579e and JEV No. 100-110-56100o. The amount of P7,849,471.50 is now included in RPCPPE. The City is in the process of coordinating with the Registry of Deeds to ensure completion of the said records. The City Accounting Office is in the process of reconciling the Schedule and GL balances. One-time cleansing for PPE account is being considered.
observations.reason: There are still land items that does not have TCT. The City Accounting Office is still in the process of reconciling the Schedule and GL balances. If exerting best efforts to locate the documents and records prove futile, consideration for write-off per COA Circular No.2020-006 is being considered.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Accounting Office to exert more efforts in gathering documents and records to expedite the process of verification activity to mobilize the disposal of unserviceable properties and recognize the related impairment loss; and b. City General Services Office to: b.1 coordinate with the Accounting Office to provide the needed details/specifications in the IIRUP; and b.2 while waiting disposal, ensure that properties for disposal are located in the protected against the elements of nature to prevent further deterioration.
observations.managementAction: The City Government is currently in the process of gathering the necessary documents to complete the details/specifications in the IIRUP. The GSO has coordinated with the Accounting Office as regards the needed details. Some of the property are located in the City’s Motorpool area. The GSO will ensure protection against the elements while awaiting disposition.
observations.reason: Initial list of IIRUP for CY 2021 and prior years was the only available record on file. The City Government is in the process of gathering documents to support disposal. Some of the property are in the Motorpool area; additional details are still being compiled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. conduct a thorough review and analysis of all outstanding balances Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees accounts and if warranted, revert to the General Fund all outstanding payables aged two years and above pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445; b. record the adjusting entries by drawing a JEV to properly reclassify the accounts by Debiting Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees accounts by P75,187,673.37 and P2,732,790.98, respectively, and crediting the affected accounts totaling P77,920,464.35; and c. exert efforts to locate documents relative to the negative/abnormal balance in order to determine the cause and initiate corrections/adjustments to fairly present the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The dormant/outstanding balances were reverted. JEVs were drawn for reversion and adjustments: JEV No. GJ 001-21-04-100, JEV No. 032-021-12-100, JEV No. 002-021-06-100, JEV No. 100-2102-021 and JEV No. 100-2102-019. City Accounting Office reverted balances per City Ordinance Nos. 2021-994 and 2021-1025. The City is exerting efforts to locate supporting documents for remaining negative/abnormal balances.
observations.reason: Reversions were processed and adjustments recorded; some negative/abnormal balances remain under analysis and reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the: a. City Social Welfare and Development Office as well as the Procurement Office to immediately prepare and submit to the Audit Team all the required documents specifically the distribution list for the SAP distribution as required under Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445; b. CSWD Office together with other concerned offices to formulate clear guidelines in the determination of the amount of financial assistance, specifically for burial to come out with a uniform rate to avoid subjective evaluation in the determination of the amount of financial assistance.
observations.managementAction: A Certification was given by the CSWD assuring beneficiaries are found eligible for Financial Assistance. The distribution list of beneficiaries were submitted on December 28, 2021 and additional documentation was submitted on March 23, 2021. The CSWD is working to establish clear guidelines for determination of assistance amounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. prepare the Distribution list/Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances as well as the required one-time report for in-kind donations and submit to the NDRRMC through the Office of Civil Defense (OCD), copy furnished the audit team; and b. ensure proper monitoring of all donations in-kind received from all sources by maintaining separate records of receipts and issuance/distribution thereof.
observations.managementAction: The City Social Welfare and Development Office provided the distribution lists and the required documents to the OCD for submission to the NDRRMC; copies of documents were submitted to the Audit Team on December 28, 2021 and additional documentation was submitted on March 23, 2021. Monitoring of donations in-kind is being conducted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor require the City Planning and Development Officer to: a. properly plan the projects to be executed that are responsive to the needs of the time especially during pandemic; and b. observe proper timing and planning in the realignment of the projects to ensure timely implementation of the existing projects. Timelines in the application of the necessary permits should be considered in project planning.
observations.managementAction: Management noted that realignment was done to respond to current needs and that they will be more cautious in project timing and planning. They indicated that timeliness of necessary permits and large volume of projects affected implementation. They will adhere to guidelines and improve planning.
observations.reason: Timeliness of implementation was affected by volume of projects and timing of required permits; corrective measures being instituted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City Treasurer to ensure that all accountable officers of the City be properly bonded pursuant to the provisions of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009.
observations.managementAction: All accountable officers were properly bonded for CY 2021 and documentation was provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to prepare and submit the required PMR and post in the website of the City as prescribed under Section 1 of EO No. 662, s. 2007, as amended.
observations.managementAction: BAC complied and submitted PMR for CY 2021: 1st Semester - August 2, 2021; 2nd Semester - February 2, 2022. The PMR was posted in the City website as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that BAC strictly comply with the requirement of conduct of bid invitation in all stages of bidding in all stages of procurement to enhance transparency in the procurement process of the City and include the information on the dates pertaining to the following: 1. Opening of Bids; 2. Bid Evaluation Meeting and Approval; and 3. Post-Qualification.
observations.managementAction: BAC complied and submitted the related documents to the Audit Team which provides details regarding the dates for opening of bids, evaluation meetings and post-qualification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor consider the budget of the City Division Office (CDO), DepEd in the preparation of the SEF budget to ensure that SEF budget during the year should be net of CDO budget pursuant to Section 4.1 of the DepEd/DBM/DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
observations.managementAction: The City provided certifications and indicated compliance that the SEF expenditures were coordinated with DepEd and adjustments were made. Records were furnished to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor require the OIC-City Economic Enterprise Office to: a. enforce the provision of the contract on the timely payment of rent and the imposition of penalties and surcharges. Any moratorium/relaxation of payment should be documented and approved by the City Mayor; b. send demand letters to all stall owners with overdue accounts including power and water bills, if no moratorium; and c. maintain records for each stall/unit owners for the monitoring of payments and submit record of monthly receivables to the Accounting Office for recording in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Monitoring of stall owners' outstanding balances as of December 31, 2021 was submitted to the Audit Team. Demand letters were issued where appropriate and statuses were updated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-City Accountant to: a. assign personnel to work specifically on the reconciliation of the accounts Due to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth to identify the status/composition and other details of the unremitted balance and remit immediately to the various agencies, if warranted; b. make representations with the concerned officials of the GSIS, BIR, Pag-IBIG Fund and PhilHealth as regards the settlement of the prior years’ unremitted withheld taxes/contributions/payments to avoid imposition of interests, surcharges and penalties; and c. henceforth, strictly adhere with the prescribed period of remittances of the withheld taxes, contributions/loan payments to the various agencies to avoid the payment of interests, surcharges and penalties and the non-granting of loans and/or benefits to the City officials and employees.
observations.managementAction: Coordination with concerned agencies is ongoing. Some prior balances were subsequently remitted. Deadlines for remittances are being met and in many cases remittances were accomplished ahead of time. Reconciliation activities are ongoing for prior years.
observations.reason: There are still unreconciled / unremitted balances relating to prior years. Reconciliation and coordination with respective agencies are ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management revisit its GAD PPAs, providing more attention and study on how to maximize the utilization of the GAD fund in order to attain the objectives for which the funds is intended especially during pandemic. Fund utilization should be maximized and a catch-up plan to maximize GAD Fund utilization be formulated.
observations.managementAction: The City stated they formulate GPB within mandates and are integrating various PPAs into regular PPAs to avoid duplication; some livelihood projects were temporarily suspended to avoid duplication. Continuous assessment is ongoing and quarterly monitoring activities will be intensified.
observations.reason: GAD fund utilization rate was only 51.55% despite reassessments; some PPAs integrated into other programs and some suspended to avoid duplication.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineering Office and the City Disaster and Risk Reduction Management Office (CDRRMO) to: a. explore all avenues to relocate the illegal settlers in the proposed site; or b. consider the possibility of other project sites to ensure project implementation with the initial identified site; and c. henceforth, conduct feasibility study first before finalizing project proposals specifically on the proposed site to avoid delay in project implementation.
observations.managementAction: Management stated relocation of informal settlers is ongoing and the City is awaiting resolution on informal settlers; the project was bidded as of CY 2022 and other issues are being addressed. Management will be more cautious in identifying project sites and conducting feasibility studies.
observations.reason: Demolition of houses in the project site was postponed due to pandemic and pending resolution on informal settlers; project bidding progressed in CY 2022 and remaining issues are being addressed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City Officials to strictly observe the provisions of Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on timely submission of approved POs/Contracts with supporting documents to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: BAC and concerned offices complied with submission of the required documents and explained causes of delays. Corrective measures were implemented to ensure timelier submission in the future.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to:
1. coordinate with the City Engineer to identify and reconcile the property, plant
and equipment that have been improved, rehabilitated, and repaired, then
derecognized the amount of assets equivalent to the replaced parts or any
other necessary adjustments, to comply with the Paragraph 85 of IPSAS 17;
2. seek the assistance of the City Engineering Office to identify the
improved/replaced portion of the City Hall Building and derecognize the cost
of the improved/replaced parts. Likewise, continue to reconcile, analyze and
investigate, together with General Services Office, the other cause/s of
discrepancies between the book balance and the RPCPPE and make
necessary adjustments, if warranted;
3. review the Schedule of PPE and draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to take
up the adjustments in the Depreciation Expense and Accumulated
Depreciation accounts in the amount of P919,360.78 for the PPE accounts
affected as presented in Annex I, to fairly present the financial statements
in accordance with PAG 3 of IPSAS 17;
4. provide residual value estimates to at least five percent (5%) on the identified
PPEs with no residual value provision in accordance with PAG 6 of IPSAS
17; and
5. exert efforts to locate documents and if turned futile, request for authority to
write-off the unreconciled/undocumented accounts without details by
following COA Circular 2020-006 on the “Guidelines and Procedures in the
Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE),
Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account
Balances of Government Agencies” to remove negative balances that
understates the gross amount of PPE.

b. City General Services Officer to:
1. coordinate with the Audit Team on the submission of documents necessary
for the Relief of Accountability applied for the burned City Library Building;
and
2. coordinate with City Accounting Office (CAO) for the details of the
unreconciled accounts and subsequently, analyze and determine the other
causes of difference between the books and RPCPPE to ensure
reconciliation of both accounting and property records.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to base the computation of interest on the maturity dates
indicated in the Certificate of Time Deposits and record the earned interest only,
instead of the interest for the whole duration of the time deposit to ensure
correct computation; and
b. OIC-City Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher/s (JEVs) to adjust the
recorded unearned interest in the amount of P791,401.45 and the unrecorded
credit memo amounting P3,976,366.00 for the collection from PhilHealth to fairly
present the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to draw a JEV to record the unrecorded CIP with a debit to
CIP-Infrastructure Assets P31,431,464.47 and CIP-Buildings and Other
Structures P38,364,000.00 and credit Accounts Payable by the same amount of
P69,795,4646.47. Likewise, adjust the erroneous entry in the CIP by debiting
Repairs and Maintenance - Infrastructure Assets and crediting CIP by the same
amount of P10,738,612.76;
b. Office of the OIC-City Accountant and Office of the City Engineer to coordinate
with each other to compile the records/documents of all ongoing projects to
facilitate recording of additions and adjustments, under the appropriate asset
accounts for fair presentation of financial statement; and
c. General Services Office to henceforth, include Construction in Progress
account in the conduct of physical count of PPE and preparation of RPCPPE.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City General Services Officer to:
 transfer the amount of P2,189,269.46 from the land account to Road Network
account;
 include the amount of P7,849,471.50 representing 15% expropriation deposit
to the cost of the land;
 reconciliation of the RPCPPE with Accounting records to ensure correct
balances;
 exert effort to locate the missing TCTs for verification/validation and submit
to the Audit Team certified copies of the same; and
 henceforth, ensure that the RPCPPE contains all the details of the property
specifically TCT Numbers for easy reference.

b. OIC-City Accountant to:
 assign personnel to gather documents and records relative to the
P15,806,356.62 difference between the Schedule and GL balances. If
exerting best efforts to locate the documents and records prove futile,
consider the write-off of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular 2020-
006 on the “Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at
Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-
Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies”.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accounting Office to exert more efforts in gathering documents and records
to expedite the verification activity to mobilize the disposal of unserviceable
properties and recognize the related impairment loss; and
b. City General Services Office:
 coordinate with the Accounting Office to provide the needed
details/specifications in the IIRUP; and
 while waiting disposal, ensure that properties for disposal are protected
against the elements of nature to prevent further deterioration.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-City Accountant
to:

a. conduct a thorough review and analysis of all outstanding Accounts Payable
and Due to Officers and Employees accounts and if warranted, revert to the
General Fund all dormant and long outstanding payables aged two years and
above pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445;
b. record the adjusting entries by drawing a JEV to properly reclassify the
accounts by Debiting Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees
accounts by P75,187,673.37 and P2,732,790.98, respectively and crediting the
following accounts:
Other Payables P53,452,523.05
Leave Benefits Payable 13,115,158.06
Due to Officers and Employees 2,192,089.75
Accounts Payable 20,450.95
Due to NGAs 2,525,861.41
Due to NGOs/Pos 3,721,930.87
Due to GOCCs 920,639.75
Due to PhilHealth 5,000.00
Due to PagIbig 159,450.00
Due to GSIS 194,630.79
Due to LGUs 1,612,729.72
Total P77,920,464.35
c. exert efforts to locate documents and records relative to the negative/abnormal
balance in order to determine the cause of the negative balances and initiate
corrections/adjustments to fairly present the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the concerned personnel to take appropriate action against the persons responsible for the late remittances and to ensure strict compliance with Section 247 of the National Internal Revenue Code of the Philippines. Recoveries shall be pursued from responsible persons and appropriate disciplinary/legal action shall be instituted, as warranted.
observations.managementAction: Preparation of representation to the BIR for reconsideration on the imposition of penalties is in process and appropriate action against concerned personnel is being pursued. Management stated that corrective measures will be taken against responsible personnel.
observations.reason: The City is still awaiting reply from the BIR on the request for reconsideration; appropriate action against concerned personnel is still pending.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior years audit recommendations that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to:

a.1 continue to reconcile, analyze and investigate the noted discrepancy
between the book balance and the RPCPPE to fully identify the
errors/deficiencies that contributed to the noted variance, and make the
necessary adjustments;

a.2 exert more effort to retrieve documents to support the undocumented
forwarded balances without detail in the General Ledgers (GLs);

a.3 adjust the residual value estimates to at least five percent in accordance
with Paragraph 6 of IPSAS 17;

a.4 make the necessary adjustment to reclassify the Construction in Progress -
Infrastructure Assets account under the Trust Fund to Water Supply System
by P3,999,324.83;

a.5 review supporting documents and schedules of the GL and Subsidiary
Ledger (SL) balances of all PPE accounts to determine the variance between
the GL and Lapsing Schedule to hasten reconciliation of GL, SL and Lapsing
Schedule; and

a.6 prepare and submit the necessary documents to properly process the
derecognition of the unserviceable properties as stated under Section 379
of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 and Commission on Audit (COA)
Memorandum No. 88-569 dated August 12, 1988; and

b. City General Services Office (GSO) to:

b.1 ensure the accuracy and completeness of the RPCPPE to hasten
reconciliation; and

b.2 ensure that the RPCPPE and accounting records are reconciled.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to:

 correct the erroneous recording of Cash in Bank - LCCA and Interest
Receivable accounts by P1,294,432.76;
 book up the unrecorded disbursements of P987,590.15;
 ensure that the reported Cash in Bank balances are reconciled with the bank
and Check Disbursement Records maintained by the City Treasurers Office;
 and
 record the unrecorded bank balance of P27,740.69 with the LBP under
Account No. 1411-1428-64.

b. OIC-City Treasurer to:
 coordinate with the OIC-City Accountant to reconcile their books at least
every quarter to avoid unrecorded disbursement; and
 inquire the status and other details of the P27,740.69 deposit with the LBP
under Account No. 1411-1428-64 and close the account if no longer
needed.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. OIC-City Accountant to:

a.1 prepare Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the understatement of Amortization
Expenses and Accumulated Amortization and the overstatement of
Accumulated Surplus and Intangible Assets - Computer Software accounts;
and henceforth,

a.2 amortize the cost of the software over its useful life;

a.3 derecognize the cost of unserviceable computer software in the amount of
P17,222,685.30 in accordance with Section 111 of IPSASs 31 on retirement
and disposal of intangible assets; and

a.4 reclassify the Intangible Assets - Computer Software account to Other
Machinery and Equipment in the amount of P175,000.00 for the installation
cost of CCTV; and

b. OIC-City Accountant and OIC-GSO to reconcile their records, and make the
necessary adjustments for the unreconciled items.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant to:

a. adjust the Interest Receivable and Interest Income accounts by the same
amount of P296,949.40 to correct the overstatement; and henceforth,

b. ensure that interest income are only recognized when realized and earned, in
compliance with the revenue recognition principle using accrual accounting;
and

c. correctly compute the interest income by using the interest rate based on the
most recent Certificate of Time Deposit.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. Procurement Office to monitor the balances of undelivered items requested per
APR and identify which APR balance is no longer needed; and thereafter,
request PS-DBM to apply prior yearss APR balances that are no longer
needed to current year APRs before making new advances; and

b. OIC-City Accountant to ensure that all prior yearss APR balances that are no
longer needed are applied first to current year APRs before processing and
signing the disbursement voucher/s pertaining to the payment of advances to
PS-DBM.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-City Accountant to:

a. exhaust all means to determine the composition and other details of the prior
years obligations and remit immediately to the BIR, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth the
total amount due to the said agencies;

b. prioritize the reconciliation of the Due to GSIS account to identify the
status/composition and other details of the unremitted balance and remit
immediately to the GSIS;

c. make representations with the concerned officials of the GSIS, BIR, Pag-IBIG
Fund and PhilHealth as regards the settlement of the prior yearss unremitted
withheld taxes/contributions/payments to avoid the imposition of interests,
surcharges and penalties; and henceforth,

d. strictly adhere to the prescribed period of remittances of the withheld taxes,
contributions/loan payments to the various agencies to avoid the payment of
interests, surcharges and penalties and the non-granting of loans and/or
benefits to the City officials and employees.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-General Services Officer to
strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002
dated May 31, 2018 for the guidelines in the insurance of government properties
with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City Officials to strictly
observe the provisions of Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on timely
submission of approved POs/Contracts with supporting documents to the Audit
Team.

We recommended, and Management agreed, that the OIC-City Treasurer submit to
the Audit Team the CRAAF in accordance with the abovementioned rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials to prepare
the required report on time, and provide the assigned Supervising Auditor and
Audit Team Leader with a list of all ongoing PPAs, and those that are to be
implemented during the year, for audit validation and verification. Moreover,
comply strictly with the provisions of the afore-cited regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. instruct the City Budget Officer to replenish the amount from the General Fund
(GF) to the SEF for the inappropriate expenditures in SEF; and henceforth,

b. disapprove any disbursement which are not included in the list of allowable
expenditures chargeable against the SEF.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer and the City Planning
and Development Officer to:

a. ensure early implementation of PPAs to achieve 100 percent implementation of
PPAs during the year, considering that the funds are available for these
activities;

b. regularly coordinate with each other to ensure that the PPAs are undertaken
within target dates; and

c. see to it that funds are expended and utilized in accordance with the guidelines
on the appropriation and utilization of the 20 percent DF, pursuant to Section
3.2 (Economic Development) of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22,
2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LCPC to ensure that all PPAs in the
Annual Work and Financial Plan are among those enumerated in Item II of DILG
MC No. 2012-120, and prepare and furnish the Audit Team copies of reports
required under Item II(1) of the same MC.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to
facilitate the establishment of the GAD database as basis for planning and
programming of PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to continue to
develop and implement GAD-related programs/projects/activities with advocacy on
women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare of women,
and ensure that planned GAD PPAs are all implemented based on their approved
GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (CDRRMO) to regularly
monitor and evaluate the utilization of LDRRMF based on the Programs,
Projects and Activities (PPAs) listed in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP);

b. CDRRMO to coordinate with the City Engineer in ensuring that all programmed
infrastructure projects under the LDRRMF are monitored and completely
implemented; and

c. CDRRMO to coordinate with the OIC-City Accountant and City Budget Officer
in monitoring the utilization of the LDRRMF-Continuing Appropriation and
Special Trust Fund (STF) to ensure complete or increased fund utilization.

observations.managementAction: Management, thru the OIC-City Accountant, OIC-Procurement Service Officer and City General Services Officer, assured that they will comply with the audit recommendations of the Audit Team. Moreover, they are still in the process of preparing the necessary documentary requirements for the processing of disposal of the unserviceable properties, as well as the conduct of market study to determine the cost or value of the properties subject for disposal. They committed that after the completion of the requirements and evaluation of the Audit Team, the subject properties will be immediately scheduled for disposal, and thereafter be derecognized in the books.
observations.reason: CY 2019 Inventory count of PPEs is still incomplete and reconciliation of Physical Count of PPEs is still not reconciled with Accounting.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the City Mayor direct the:
a. OIC-City Treasurer to immediately transmit all paid disbursement vouchers with their supporting documents to the City Accountant’s Office; and
b. OIC-City Accountant to adjust the identified reconciling items in the City’s books to come up with the correct balance of the Cash in Bank account. Gather all supporting documents to reconcile the bank and book balances.
observations.managementAction: Expedited transmittal of the subject disbursement vouchers and their supporting documents to the City Accountant’s Office; and All identified deficiencies in the Cash Account of the City has been reconciled and adjusted in the books.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant to:
a. record only obligation with proper and complete documentation; and
b. make the necessary adjusting entries and reclassifying the misclassified claims to their proper accounts. Also request authority for reversion of those payables without valid documentation that have been outstanding for two years or more to Government Equity of the General Fund so that the City can use the available funds for other priority projects.
observations.managementAction: Assessment of highlighted Accounts Payable were monitored. Adjusting Journal Entry to reclassify accounts was effected through Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 100-1906-2533. The reversion of those payables without valid documentation to the Sangguniang Panlungsod was completed and amounts payable to PLDT, Inc. and Petron Corp. were reverted through enactment of Sanguniang Panlungsod.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Focal Person concerned to maximize the use of funds received from various NGAs for the immediate implementation of the prioritized PPAs and to remit to the fund from source agencies all the unused balance of funds so that the amount could be immediately used in the City for other programs/projects; and
b. OIC-City Treasurer, in coordination with the OIC-Accountant, to remit to the BTr/source agencies all the unused balance of funds and to relieve the City from any accountability/liability for the said unused funds.
observations.managementAction: The focal persons maximized the use of the funds on the local DRRMF for disaster mitigation programs. The City submitted requests for refund of various fund transfers to the BTr and to relieve the City from accountability and liability for the unused funds. The sum of P2,010,694.91 pertaining to CYs 2016 to 2018 APR balances under the General Fund were requested to be refunded, while the CY 2019 balance amounting to P1,088,987.51 was sought to be applied for current year purchases. Request for refund for the P259,940.00 balances under SEF from CY 2018 is currently in process.

Sent collection letters to the borrowers and cooperatives, and to the concerned employees. Assessment of long outstanding/dormant balances of accounts for possibility of write-off is in process. Reprocessing of request for write-off for the unaccounted non-moving payables that were carried over from prior years’ financial statements, previously filed by the Previous Administrator to the Office of the Auditor will be initiated. Accountable Officers were initiated to be withheld of their remaining emoluments. Compliance with MOAs are being monitored.
observations.reason: Analysis and retrieval procedures are currently in process. Assessment of long outstanding/dormant balances of accounts for possibility of write-off is currently in process.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor direct the:
a. OIC-City Accountant to exhaust all means to determine the composition and other details of the prior year’s obligations and remit immediately to the BIR, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth the total amount due to the said agencies;
b. OIC-City Accountant to make the necessary adjustment in the City’s books for the under recording of remittance of tax withheld to the BIR in the amount of P134,413.00; and
c. OIC-City Accountant and OIC-City Treasurer to exert efforts in the reconciliation of records and make the necessary representation with the concerned agencies in order to effect remittances and avoid penalties, surcharges and inconvenience to the employees.
observations.managementAction: Initiated analysis and retrieval of records; compliance personnel assigned for the job; coordination with GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth is ongoing. Any unremitted amount will be immediately processed for payment to the aforesaid agencies. For GSIS remittances, coordination with GSIS is in process to resolve clarificatory items. Preparation of necessary adjusting entries is ongoing.
observations.reason: Analysis and retrieval of documents/proof are still ongoing; various clarificatory items with GSIS and other agencies require resolution before final remittance.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to closely monitor and vigorously pursue the timely and full implementation of all the programs and activities embodied in the 20 percent Development Fund so that the benefits would be delivered to its intended beneficiaries at the soonest possible time.
observations.managementAction: An increase in the utilization rate of the 20 percent Development Fund was noted from 75.79 percent in CY 2018 to 86 percent in CY 2019. Various PPAs were still not implemented; corrective measures and closer coordination among concerned offices are being undertaken.
observations.reason: Significant increase in the utilization rate has been achieved but some PPAs remain not implemented; further coordination and monitoring required.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. CSWDO to ensure the SFP is implemented immediately upon receipt of funds in accordance with the provisions of the SIA and its Project Specific Proposal; and
b. OIC-City Accountant, in coordination with CSWDO to submit a copy of the Fund Utilization Report and Nutritional Status of the Beneficiaries to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Supplemental Feeding Program (SFP) was implemented on April 16, 2019, the last day of the program. The CSWDO submitted the Fund Utilization Report and Nutritional Status of the Beneficiaries to the Audit Team thereafter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and the SWMB convene regularly to ensure that the review of the Solid Waste Management Plan will be conducted on time or as the need arises, and monitor the compliance with the provisions of Section 4(g), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The City conducted reviews and monitoring through the City Environment and Waste Management Office (CEWMO) and SWMB. Meetings are being regularly conducted and compliance improved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant to:
a. submit immediately the subject disbursement vouchers and their supporting vouchers and documents to the Audit Team to facilitate review and verification of the same; and
b. ensure that the monthly reports and their supporting documents are completely and regularly submitted to the Audit Team within the period prescribed by the mentioned regulations.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office submitted the requested documents and implemented measures to improve timely submission. Some monthly reports were slightly delayed but corrective measures are ongoing.
observations.reason: Some financial reports and supporting documents were submitted with slight delays; continuous improvement in submission timeliness is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Budget Officer to stop the practice of charging expenses not related to LCPC against the LCPC funds; and
b. City Accountant and LCPC to monitor the charging of disbursements under LCPC, pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9344 and Item II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120, and to submit reports on utilization of the said fund on a quarterly basis.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the transactions will be reviewed and that an Appropriation Ordinance to effect replenishment of fund from the General Fund to SEF for the aforesaid transactions is currently in process. Reconciliation and monitoring procedures are being instituted.
observations.reason: Some PPAs were still charged to LCPC Fund; replenishment ordinance in process and further monitoring to prevent recurrence is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Planning and Development Officer and GAD Focal Point System to establish the GAD Database and to undertake capacity building for personnel in formulation of GAD and the conversion of available data into GAD Database;
b. GAD Focal Point Person to ensure that budget appropriation of all GAD PPAs stated in the City GPB are approved by the Sanggunian, and submit copy to the Audit Team within the required period; and
c. GAD Focal Person to submit Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team within five working days from the end of January of the preceding year.
observations.managementAction: The City initiated actions to establish the GAD Database and conducted capacity building. As of May 2020, the City established its GAD Database and submitted the same to the Audit Team on June 19, 2020. Communication with various National Government Agencies for complementary data has been initiated.
observations.reason: The formulation and completion of the GAD Database is underway; some data still being gathered from national agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. maximize the use of available resources on the local DRRMF for disaster mitigation and preparedness in order to build the resilience of the City and better prepare for, and lessen the impact of natural and man-made hazards/disasters and promote socio-economic growth in the City;
b. ensure that the allocation for LDRRMF is fully utilized based on the approved LDRRMFIP and to ensure that identified priority PPAs are implemented as planned, and to clearly establish the progress and success of its implementation; and
c. instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (CDRRMC) to monitor and evaluate the implementation of LDRRM plans to better prepare for, and lessen the impact of natural and man-made hazards/disasters and promote socio-economic growth in the City.
observations.managementAction: The City has taken steps to improve utilization. Closer coordination between the offices of the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management, City Engineering, City Accounting and City Budget will be implemented to regularly monitor and to further improve the utilization rate of the City for its LDRRMF. Significant increase in utilization from prior years is being pursued.
observations.reason: An increase in utilization was achieved but utilization for CY 2019 remained low (19.55%); monitoring and coordination measures are being implemented to improve utilization.

observations.recommendation:
a. Officer-in-Charge (OIC), City  Accounting  Office  to  prepare  and
         submit  the complete Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for  all
         existing bank accounts to the Auditor within the prescribed period.
         The bank statements, together with the paid checks and debit/credit
         memos shall likewise be submitted to facilitate  its  verification.
         Moreover, verify and evaluate the correctness and accuracy  of  the
         reconciling items reflected in the BRS and  prepare  the  adjusting
         entries in the ensuing month to avoid inclusion in  the  subsequent
         BRS.


      b.  Officers-in-Charge  of  the  City  Accounting  Office   and   City
         Treasurer’s Office to  conduct  periodic  reconciliation  of  their
         records  to  ensure  accuracy  in   recording.    Also,   necessary
         adjustments, if there are any, could be effected at once  to  avoid
         accumulation of the discrepancies between the two  records  and  to
         present the correct balances of the Cash in Bank  accounts  in  the
         financial statements; and


      c. OIC,  City  Treasurer’s  Office  to  transmit  immediately  to  the
         Accounting Office all the unsubmitted  paid  disbursement  vouchers
         for recording in the books  of  the  City.   Thereafter,  the  City
         Accounting  Office  should  submit   to   the   Audit   Team   the
         corresponding  DVs  for  post-audit.  Otherwise, any unjustified
         failure on the part of the official concerned to  submit  the
         required  reports  shall  be  considered  a  ground for the automatic
         suspension of payment of his salaries until he  shall  have  complied
         therewith without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may be
         instituted against him, pursuant to Section 122 of PD No. 1445        (The
         Government Auditing Code of the Philippines).

We reiterated our recommendations that  the  City  Mayor  direct
      the:


     a. Inventory Committee to complete the inventory count  to  prove  the
        existence of the City’s properties,  and  to  submit  the  required
        report thereon to the Auditor,  pursuant  to  Section  124  of  the
        MNGAS, Volume I.  Also, the inventory report should  be  reconciled
        with the Accounting and Property records. Any  discrepancies  found
        should be investigated, and the  necessary  adjustments  should  be
        made where the error was committed;


     b. Office of the City  Accountant  to  continue  exerting  efforts  in
        tracing back  prior  years’  acquisition  of  Property,  Plant  and
        Equipment to have a complete detail of the fixed asset accounts  so
        that  the  corresponding depreciation could be computed, to present
        fairly the true value of the depreciable fixed asset accounts;


     c. Accountable Officers of the  lost/destroyed  properties  to  submit
        immediately to the Auditor the request  for  relief  from  property
        accountability,   duly   supported   with    all    the    required
        documentations, so that they can be relieved  from  any  liability,
        and the cost of the items  could  be  dropped  from  the  books  of
        accounts of the City;


     d.  City  General  Services  Office  to  prepare  the  Inventory   and
        Inspection Report on Unserviceable Property (IIURP), to account for
        all unserviceable and obsolete items previously  taken  up  in  the
        books as assets, so that they  may  be  properly  disposed  of  and
        dropped from the accounts. Moreover, the disposal of property shall
        be made in accordance with COA Circular No.  89-296  dated  January
        27, 1989; and


     e. Property Officer/GSO to make certain that Property Cards are  well-
        maintained, complete and updated to facilitate the  physical  count
        of property and equipment.  Determine  the  officials/employees  to
        whom  the  equipment  were  issued,  and  issue  the  corresponding
        Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for  each  property  to
        pinpoint the direct person  accountable  and  responsible  thereon.
        Moreover, renew all ARE every three years to update  signature  and
        information like designation/position or assignment of  the  holder
        of each item listed in the RPCPPE.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the
      OIC, City Treasurer’s  Office  to  prepare  and  submit  the  required
      certified list of taxpayers and the tax collectible from each  at  the
      beginning of the year to the OIC, City Accounting Office as a basis of
      recording in the books of accounts the RPT/SET Receivables.   We  also
      recommended that the City  Mayor  require  the  OIC,  City  Accounting
      Office to maintain Subsidiary Ledger for each  taxpayer/Real  Property
      Unit (RPU) to  support  the  recorded  balances  of  said  Receivables
      accounts.

a.   undertake  all  the  remedies  to  enforce  settlement   of   the
        unliquidated cash advances under the cited COA  Circular,  and  the
        withholding  of  the  salaries  and   other   emoluments   of   the
        officials/employees who continuously  ignore,  fail  or  refuse  to
        comply with the demand be strictly imposed;


      b. send demand  letters  to  all  officials  and  employees  who  have
        unsettled obligations to the City  Government  to  liquidate  their
        cash advances;


      c.  consider also filing appropriate charges against  them  to  compel
        compliance thereof. Consider requesting for authority to write-off
        of the unliquidated cash advances of accountable officials who  are
        already  dead  or  could  no  longer  be   located,   or   if   the
        settlement/liquidation is already impossible from the COA, pursuant
        to COA Circular No. 2016-005.


            Henceforth, see to it that the above-cited  regulations  on  the
      grant,   utilization   and   liquidation   of   cash   advances,
      particularly the period within which to liquidate the  same   are
      strictly and continuously enforced to protect the government funds from the
      risk of possible loss through misuse or misappropriation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:


      a. CGSO to coordinate with the City Engineer to    prepare  the  road
         map of  the  City  to  facilitate  the  preparation  of  a  proper
         inventory and reporting of the City’s road network system;


      b. City Engineer to provide the City Accountant and the CGSO with the
         complete description and cost segregation of road  components  for
         road projects; and thereafter,


      c.  OIC-City  Accountant  to  record  the  amount  of  road  networks
         equivalent to the required transition provision  percentage  every
         year starting 2016 up to 2019.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:


     a.  City  Engineer  and  City  Planning  and  Development  Officer  to
        integrate  the  Construction  Safety  and  Health  Program  in  the
        computation of construction costs of all projects to be implemented
        by the City pursuant to DOLE Department Order No. 13, to ensure the
        protection and welfare of the workers at the construction  site  as
        well as the general public passing around the  construction  sites;
        and


     b. Bids and Awards Committee  to  require  interested  Contractors  to
        submit DOLE-approved CSHP to ensure the  latter’s  compliance  with
        Occupational Safety and Health Standards set by the DOLE.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct  the
      concerned officials to prepare the required report on time and  provide
      the assigned Supervising Auditor and Audit  Team  Leader  with  a  list
      of all ongoing PPAs, and those that are to be  implemented  during the
      year, for audit validation and verification.  Moreover, comply  strictly
      with the provisions of the afore-cited regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the  Officer-in-Charge
      (OIC), City Accounting Office to:


     a. prepare the  necessary  adjusting  entry  to  revert  the  Accounts
        Payable of P399,001.11 to Government Equity, in order to  eliminate
        the erroneous recognition of liability in the  books  of  accounts;
        and


     b. ensure that the  recognition  of  expense  for  prepaid   insurance
        premiums of City’s properties  is compliant with the  provisions  of
        PPSAS  2  for  fair  presentation  of  accounts  in  the  financial
        statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board to:


     a. stop charging the expenses for honoraria of the asatidz and  school
        officials under Madrasah Education Program  from  SEF,  and  ensure
        that SEF is utilized for which the fund was  established,  if  not,
        any deviations from the purpose of this fund will be disallowed  in
        audit;


     b. conform strictly with the provisions of DepEd Order No. 58, s. 2016
        and consider only the  programs/projects/activities  chargeable  to
        SEF in the preparation of budget to optimize the use of such  funds
        for its intended purposes; and


     c. require the asatidz concerned to refund  immediately  to  the  City
        Government the double payment of honoraria, otherwise, this will be
        disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the concerned officers of the City Planning and
      Development and Budget Offices avoid lump-sum appropriation and identify
      specific PPAs in the AIP and allocation  of  budget  to  be  funded
      under the explicit areas of development/office indicating the  corresponding
      project cost, necessary fund flows to approximate the reasonable  timing  in
      the release of funds and period of implementation.

We  recommended  that  the  City  Mayor  direct  the  OIC,  City
      Accounting Office to:

     a. submit immediately the above reports and their supporting documents
        to the Office of the Auditor to facilitate review and  verification
        of the same. Otherwise, the corresponding Notice of Suspension  and
        Notice of Charge shall be issued for its non-submission; and


     b. ensure that the reports and supporting documents submitted  by the
        accountable officers are  immediately  recorded  in  the  books  of
        accounts of the City and submitted within the prescribed period  as
        required under COA Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2009-006. If  not,  any
        unjustified failure on the part of the official concerned to submit
        the required reports shall be considered a ground for the automatic
        suspension of payment of his salaries until he shall have  complied
        therewith without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may  be
        instituted against him, pursuant to Section 122 of PD No. 1445 (The
        Government Auditing Code of the Philippines).

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Treasurer’s Office to prepare and submit the required certified list of taxpayers and the tax collectible from each at the beginning of the year to the OIC, City Accounting Office as the basis of recording the RPT/SET Receivables in the books of accounts. It should be emphasized that there is a need to provide an accurate and reliable basis of the actual RPT/SET Receivables from taxpayers, not only for a fair presentation of the actual financial position of the City, but also to provide Management with accurate information needed in its decision-making.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge, City Accounting Office to prepare the required adjusting journal entries for all valid reconciling items totaling P4,361,299.15, to present fairly the correct balance of Cash in Bank - Local Cur-rency, Current Account in the Financial Statements.
	
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Treasurer’s Office to determine the cause/s of the delay in the transmittal of paid disbursement vouchers (DVs) by the Treasurer’s Office to the Accounting Office, and find solution to correct the same. Likewise, ensure that all paid DVs are immediately forwarded to the Accounting Office for proper recording in the books of accounts to present the true balance of Cash in Bank accounts in the Financial Statements.  See to it that the details of all reconciling items presented in the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRSs) are properly and completely stated and supported with complete documents, such as debit memo and credit memo, to facilitate the verification of the same by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC), City Accounting Office to ensure that the recognition of interest income from time deposits is compliant with the provisions of PPSAS 2 for fair presentation of accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Treasurer’s Office to deposit intact and regularly all collections with the depository bank, to safeguard government funds against theft or loss, pursuant to Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct concerned officials to undertake all the remedies to enforce settlement of the unliquidated cash advances under the above COA Circular, and the withholding  of  the salaries and other emoluments of the officials/employees who continuously  ignore, fail or refuse to comply with the demand should be strictly imposed.  Also, request assistance from the heads of the national government agencies who have jurisdictions over the national government employees with unliquidated cash advances in demanding the immediate liquidation thereof. Demand letters should be sent to all officials and employees who have unsettled obligations to the City Government to liquidate their cash advances. Consider also filing appropriate charges against them to compel compliance thereof. Consider requesting for the write-off of the unliquidated cash advances of accountable officials who are already dead or could no longer be located, or if the settlement/liquidation is already impossible from the Commission on Audit.

Henceforth, see to it that the regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances are strictly and continuously enforced, particularly the period within which to liquidate the same as prescribed under the above regulation, to protect the government funds to risk of possible loss through misuse or misappropriation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer (GSO) to insure all properties of the City with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS, so that in case of loss or destruction of the same, the City could at least recover the cost of these assets.

	We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Budget Office to see to it that funds for the purpose of insuring the properties are included in the annual budget of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials to prepare the required report on time and provide the assigned Supervising Auditor and Audit Team Leader with a list of all ongoing PPAs, and those that are to be implemented during the year, for audit validation and verification.  Moreover, comply strictly with the provisions of the aforecited regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials to submit copies of the contracts and purchase orders within five days from issuance as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001, together with the pertinent documents, which are deemed necessary in the conduct of auditorial review, and in the technical evaluation thereof.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, Procurement Services Office to strictly comply with the requirements on the procurement process as provided under COA Circular No. 96-010.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Accounting Office strictly comply with the provisions of the PPSAS 2 for fair presentation of accounts in the Financial Statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Assessor to prepare the required list of idle lands and submit the same to the City Treasurer to facilitate the computation and imposition of the additional ad valorem tax to generate additional income for the City Government. Likewise, the City Assessor should always keep an updated list of idle lands within the territorial jurisdiction of the City of Antipolo, in compliance with the above guidelines on idle lands tax.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials to submit to the Audit Team the basis of 3.5% handling fee being charged against the City Government in connection to the procurement of fuel thru Petron Fleet Card, to support the propriety and regularity of the disbursement made, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued to cause the refund of payments made.  In addition, the OIC, City Procurement Services Office should be cognizant that Petron Fleet Card has also product rebates and loyalty features that are given to Petron valued customers that could be availed of by the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials to stop the granting of Transportation Allowances to City officials who are assigned government motor vehicles, pursuant to the provisions of LBC No. 102, as amended.

	Also, require the City officials concerned to refund the Transportation Allowances granted to them during the year, otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Treasurer’s Office to stop the practice of designating casual employees as Collecting Officer or as a custodian of government funds, pursuant to Section 65 of the GAAM, Volume I, to protect the government funds against possible loss through misuse or misappropriation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

OIC, City  Accounting  Office to submit to the Audit Team  all the lacking documents to substantiate the disbursements made, as well as, to avoid audit suspensions. Likewise, ensure that the Accounting Office review the completeness of the supporting documents, as required under Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing the payment;

City officials concerned  to ensure  that  all  documentary  requirements, including the items to be included in the Memorandum of Agreement as provided under COA Circular No. 2007-001, are strictly complied with prior to the release of funds to NGOs/POs including the proper accounting thereof; and 

OIC, City Treasurer’s Office to remit immediately the unexpended balance of the said fund amounting to  P2,100.00 to the DSWD Field Office IV-A.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the City Mayor:

a. direct the OIC-City Accounting Office to submit immediately the above reports and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor to facilitate review and verification of the same.  Henceforth, ensure that the financial reports and documents are submitted within the prescribed period as required under COA Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2009-006.  Otherwise, any unjustified failure on the part of the official concerned to submit the required reports shall be considered a ground for the automatic suspension of payment of her salaries until she shall have complied therewith without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may be instituted against her, pursuant to Section 122 of PD No. 1445 (The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines); and

b. determine the cause/s of the delay in the submission of the accounts of the City to the Auditor and find solutions thereto.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials to evaluate the need for these unexpended balances of funds from various source agencies.  If there is still a need, make a written representation to the source agencies to use the money for other projects or purpose of the City for the benefits of its constituents. Otherwise, return the same to the concerned donors or remit it to the Bureau of the Treasury, as required under the aforecited rules and regulations.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the officials concerned to cause the return of the unexpended balance of the Priority Development Assistance Fund (PDAF) to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer to allocate no less than 5% of its total annual appropriations of GAD programs, activities and projects, in compliance with PWC, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

	We also recommended that the City Mayor ensure that programs, plans and activities are implemented in accordance with the approved GAD Plan and Budget to maximize the use of allocated funds, and that all issues on gender mainstreaming are appropriately resolved.  Further, the City, as much as possible, quantify the targets and accomplishments relative to GAD-related activities for easy monitoring, and facilitate appraisal of performance, as vital information for their decision-making.  This way, assessing the level of performance in relation to its targets could easily be done. This will also ensure that the activities are undertaken and resources are being used in accordance with the plans previously set.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC, City Accounting Office to prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF monthly in order for the OIC, LDRRMO to submit the said report to the concerned offices on the required dates, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer to appropriate fund in the General Fund to restore the amount of LDRRMF utilized for the items procured not related to disaster risk management, pursuant to NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-001, otherwise the payment shall be disallowed in audit. Further, refrain from using the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund for purposes other than those provided for under Item 5.5 of the said Circular.  The Property Officer should update the ARE issued for the two units of  motor vehicles (KIA K2700 4x4) through cancellation of the old ARE and issuance of the new one in the name of the incumbent OIC, City Procurement Service Office to transfer the accountability to the same.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the:

Property Officer to prepare and maintain inventory list of all procured and donated items based on the updated Stock Cards and Property Cards, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014. Further, periodic reconciliation should be made against accounting records.

Property Officer to update the copy of the Acknowledgement Receipts for Equipment (AREs) issued to previous OIC, CDRRMO to pinpoint accountability towards the property being issued; and

City Engineer to provide storage/stock room with adequate space to accommodate all the supplies, materials, equipment and relief goods of the CDRRMO for safekeeping.  Make sure that storage facilities are located in accessible, safe and secured areas, to respond immediately to the needs of the victims in times of calamity.

We recommended that Management cause the refund of the mentioned allowances from the concerned employees and stop further granting of the same.

	We also recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of Section 195 of the GAAM, Volume I on the releasing of payments of any claims due to government officials and employees to ensure that the same are received by the right persons.

We reiterated our recommendation that the penalties charged by the BIR to the City Government for the delayed remittance of withholding tax on compensation, goods and services be shouldered by the City officials who were responsible for the late remittance of the said taxes.  Henceforth, ensure strict compliance with Section 247 of the National Internal Revenue Code of the Philippines.
